skill.name: hooks-automation
skill.description: Guides setup of Claude Code hook automation for pre-task preparation, post-edit formatting, session state, memory coordination, Git checks, and MCP-based agent workflows.
skill.summary: Configure Claude Code hooks for coordinated automation, memory, formatting, Git checks, and session workflows.
skill.author: ruvnet

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many external command patterns, but most are Markdown examples for configuring Claude Code hooks rather than hidden executable files. The confirmed concern is medium risk: the guide recommends dynamic command hooks with interpolated tool parameters, which can enable command injection or unsafe automation if copied without validation.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Hook Commands Use Interpolated Parameters
medium_findings.description: The hook configuration examples place tool parameters inside shell command strings. File paths, Bash commands, task descriptions, and search patterns are passed into commands, which is legitimate for hook automation but can become command injection if values are not escaped and validated.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic command strings are directly visible in the hook configuration examples. The surrounding context is legitimate documentation, so the risk depends on how users install and sanitize these hooks.

medium_findings.title: Automated Git and Test Hooks Execute Local Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Git hook scripts and debugging commands that run npm, git, jq, bc, date substitution, and claude-flow commands. These examples are useful, but they expand the execution surface when installed into repository hooks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are shown in fenced documentation blocks and are not hidden payloads. They are still operational instructions that users may copy into executable hooks.

low_findings.title: Destructive Command Flag Is a Documentation Example
low_findings.description: The recursive delete pattern appears as an example command passed to a pre-bash safety checker. It targets /tmp/cache and is presented as a command to validate, not as a hidden operation performed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding section describes command safety validation and confirmation. I found no evidence that the skill itself executes this delete command automatically.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Environment File References Are Protective Examples
low_findings.description: The production environment file references are used in examples for backup, conflict checks, and blocked protected-file responses. They do not show secret extraction or transmission.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly frame sensitive files as protected targets requiring manual review. No evidence of credential reading or exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Appear Unconfirmed
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the inspected context is Markdown documentation about hooks, metrics, memory keys, and hash identifiers. No evidence found for use of MD5, SHA-1, or custom cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited areas are documentation metadata or command examples, not cryptographic implementations. Confidence is high for dismissal of inspected lines, but the static report listed many line hits.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Strings in Hook Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The command-based hook settings demonstrate shell commands assembled with runtime parameters. Marketplace users should review quoting, escaping, and confirmation behavior before enabling them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in the configuration examples and involves runtime values. The risk is contextual because these are examples rather than bundled executable files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository Hook Scripts with Command Substitution
dangerous_patterns.description: The Git hook examples use shell scripts and command substitution to gather files, commit metadata, metrics, and dates. This is common for Git automation but should be treated as executable code during review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippets are clearly marked as shell examples. They are not malicious, but they are dangerous if copied into hooks without project-specific review.

content.value_statement: Manual hook setup can make agent workflows inconsistent and hard to audit. This skill explains coordinated Claude Code hooks for task setup, formatting, memory, metrics, and Git quality gates.
actual_capabilities: Explains pre-operation and post-operation hooks for edits, Bash commands, tasks, and searches.
Shows Claude Code settings examples for command-based hook automation.
Describes session start, restore, and end workflows for preserving context.
Covers memory coordination patterns for multi-agent Claude Flow workflows.
Provides Git hook examples for validation, formatting, metrics, and quality gates.
limitations: Requires Claude Flow CLI and Claude Code hook support to be useful.
Provides documentation and examples, not a packaged hook runner.
Command examples need project-specific safety review before installation.
MCP and swarm features depend on external server configuration.

use_cases.title: Standardize Repository Hooks
use_cases.description: Create repeatable Claude Code hook patterns for formatting, validation, testing, and session reporting across repositories.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Multi-Agent Work
use_cases.description: Use task, memory, and notification hooks to keep specialized agents aligned during larger implementation work.

use_cases.title: Preserve Development Context
use_cases.description: Restore session state, store decisions, and export summaries so work can continue across Claude Code sessions.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Basic Hooks
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a minimal Claude Code hook setup for this repository. Include pre-edit validation, post-edit formatting, and session-end summaries.

prompt_templates.title: Add Git Quality Gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe Git hook plan using Claude Flow hooks for staged file validation, targeted tests, and quality reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Agent Memory
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a memory coordination workflow for backend, frontend, and test agents using Claude Code hooks and clear memory namespaces.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Hook Safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Claude Code hook configuration for command injection, unsafe file access, and excessive automation risk before I enable it.

output_examples.input: User asks for a basic hook strategy for a Node project.
output_examples.output: A short hook plan covering pre-edit syntax checks, post-edit formatting, targeted tests, and a session summary.

output_examples.input: User asks how to coordinate three agents on one feature.
output_examples.output: A workflow that assigns agents, stores shared memory under clear namespaces, and broadcasts task completion events.

output_examples.input: User asks whether a hook configuration is safe to enable.
output_examples.output: A review of dynamic command inputs, protected file handling, confirmation points, and safer quoting requirements.

best_practices: Review every command hook as executable code before enabling it in Claude Code.
Use allowlists, quoting, and confirmation for file paths, Bash commands, and task descriptions.
Keep memory keys namespaced so agents can share context without overwriting unrelated state.
anti_patterns: Do not copy dynamic shell commands into hooks without validating user-controlled parameters.
Do not enable automatic cleanup or formatting hooks on protected files without manual approval.
Do not store secrets, credentials, or sensitive production data in shared hook memory.

faq.question: Does this skill install hooks automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents hook setup patterns and command examples for Claude Code and Claude Flow.

faq.question: Which tools does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Claude Code, Claude Flow CLI, MCP tools, and compatible Codex or Claude workflows.

faq.question: Is it safe to copy the command examples directly?
faq.answer: Review them first. Dynamic command hooks can be risky if inputs are not escaped and validated.

faq.question: Can it help with Git hooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for pre-commit, post-commit, and pre-push quality workflows.

faq.question: Does it require MCP servers?
faq.answer: Core hook concepts do not require MCP, but agent coordination examples depend on MCP configuration.

faq.question: Can it preserve session context?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes session start, restore, and end hooks for memory, metrics, and summaries.
